answer:
Substantial — this fact pattern hits nearly every misclassification trigger.

**Why it looks like employment:**
- **Company laptop** — you provide the tools (behavioral/financial control)
- **Daily standups** — you control how/when she works
- **Core product, ~1 year** — integral to your business and an ongoing, indefinite relationship (key factor under the DOL's economic-realities rule)
- **Hourly pay** — no opportunity for profit/loss like a true independent business
- **30 hrs/week** — likely economically dependent on you

Under the IRS common-law test, the DOL's 2024 economic realities rule (still in effect), and Ohio's unemployment/workers' comp tests, she would very likely be found an employee.

**Exposure if reclassified:**
- Back employer payroll taxes (FICA, FUTA) + penalties and interest; IRS penalties roughly double if deemed willful
- Unpaid overtime under FLSA if she ever exceeded 40 hrs (2–3 year lookback, liquidated/double damages)
- Ohio unemployment contributions and workers' comp premiums, retroactive
- Possible benefits claims (retirement plan, health coverage eligibility) and state penalties

**What to do:** Convert her to a part-time W-2 employee (cleanest fix), or use IRS Voluntary Classification Settlement Program (VCSP) to settle past liability at ~1% of one year's wages if you haven't been audited yet. Talk to an employment attorney before you get a 1099 audit trigger — e.g., her filing Form SS-8 or an unemployment claim.
